item.title: From the archives: David Letterman on launching "The Late Show" in 1993
item.description: Ahead of the premiere of "The Late Show" in August 1993, host David Letterman sat down with "CBS This Morning" to discuss his break from his previous late night show, what viewers could expect from his CBS debut and more.

item.title: What sagging bond prices are telling us about the U.S. economy
item.description: U.S. government bonds are sagging as investors fret that hotter inflation will keep interest rate cuts on hold.

item.title: Watch: Blanche takes questions on Raúl Castro indictment
item.description: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Wednesday announced the indictment of former Cuban leader Raúl Castro and five others in connection with the Cuban military's fatal downing of two planes 30 years ago. After Blanche answered some questions about the charges, CBS News' Cristian Benavides joined with additional reporting.

item.title: Teen attackers in mosque shooting were wallowing in nihilistic hate
item.description: "These subjects did not discriminate in who they hated," said Mark Remily, special agent in charge of the FBI's San Diego Field Office.

item.title: Trump says Netanyahu will do "whatever I want him to do" on Iran
item.description: Asked what he said to Israel's leader about a decision to hold off on new Iran strikes, Trump said Netanyahu will "do whatever I want him to do."

item.title: Watch: Blanche announces indictment against Raúl Castro, 5 others over 1996 downing of planes
item.description: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced murder and conspiracy charges Wednesday against former Cuban leader Raúl Castro and five others over the downing of two planes in 1996.

item.title: Summer travelers face "vacation inflation" as airfare, gas prices rise
item.description: Gas prices, airfares, accommodations and other vacation essentials are more expensive this year compared to last year.

item.title: U.S. indicts Cuba's Raúl Castro for murder over downing of planes in 1996
item.description: Former Cuban leader Raúl Castro was indicted by a U.S. grand jury in connection with the Cuban military's fatal downing of two planes in 1996 — an escalation in the U.S. pressure campaign against the Cuban government.

item.title: Man thrown in jail for 37 days over Charlie Kirk post wins $835,000 settlement
item.description: Larry Bushart, who spent 37 days in jail over his Facebook post about the assassination of conservative activist Charlie Kirk, will get $835,000 to settle a lawsuit against Tennessee officials. CBS News' Shanelle Kaul reports.

item.title: Who's who in Cuba's leadership? Here are the names to know.
item.description: President Trump has been openly mulling a takeover of Cuba similar to the operation that toppled Venezuela's Nicolás Madoro. What it would mean for the regime's leaders remains to be seen. Here are some of the figures to watch.

item.title: Netanyahu rebukes Israeli minister for treatment of Gaza flotilla activists
item.description: Italy and France have summoned the Israeli ambassadors in their respective capitals after Itamar Ben-Gvir, his national security minister, posted the video.

item.title: U.S. indicts Cuba's Raúl Castro and 5 others
item.description: Former Cuban leader Raúl Castro and five others were indicted by a U.S. grand jury in Florida, according to court filings made public Wednesday. CBS News' Cristian Benavides, Olivia Gazis and Jake Rosen have the latest.

item.title: The story of Cuba's 1996 shootdown that led to Raúl Castro's indictment
item.description: Thirty years ago, a Cuban fighter jet shot down two civilian planes operated by Florida-based exile group Brothers to the Rescue, an incident that inflamed U.S.-Cuba relations.

item.title: Bezos says bottom half of U.S. earners should pay no federal income tax
item.description: The Amazon founder said eliminating taxes for lower-income Americans could ease financial pressure and encourage entrepreneurship.

item.title: Spy plane "dangerously intercepted" by Russian jets over Black Sea
item.description: The Russian jets intercepted the U.K. aircraft with one jet flying as close as 19 feet to the British plane's nose, officials said.

item.title: How much of your retirement income can creditors take?
item.description: There are laws that protect your retirement income from creditors, but there are also exceptions to know about.

item.title: From the archives: Dan Rather interviews Fidel Castro in 1996
item.description: In July of 1996, CBS News' Dan Rather traveled to Cuba to interview Fidel Castro. The two discussed a number of subjects about the country and Castro's life.

item.title: Rubio offers Cubans "new path" in video address
item.description: Raúl Castro and five others have been indicted by a U.S. grand jury in Florida, according to court filings made public Wednesday.

item.title: $2,500 CD vs. $2,500 high-yield savings account vs. $2,500 money market account: Which will earn more over the next year?
item.description: All three accounts can be viable for your $2,500 over the next year, but here's which one stands out the most.

item.title: Barney Frank, Massachusetts congressman for 32 years, dies at 86
item.description: Barney Frank​, a Democrat who represented Massachusetts in Congress for 32 years, has died. He was 86 years old.

item.title: Deadly measles outbreak in Bangladesh fuels concern over risks for U.S.
item.description: A CBS News medical correspondent and doctor says her "biggest concern for the World Cup is actually measles. It's not hantavirus, it is not Ebola."

item.title: 2 officers in Jan. 6 riot sue to block DOJ "anti-weaponization" fund
item.description: The $1.776 billion fund, which is part of the agreement to settle Trump's lawsuit against the IRS and Treasury Dept., is to be used to compensate those who claim that the government weaponized the legal system against them.

item.title: Which college majors offer the best long-term return on investment?
item.description: College grads outearn people without a degree within 15 years, even after paying for tuition, study finds.

item.title: 2 humpback whales swam record-breaking distance, photos reveal
item.description: The pictures represent the longest-distance ever seen between two pictures of the same humpback whale, researchers said.

item.title: Raúl Castro indictment expected to be announced in Miami today, sources say
item.description: Raúl Castro is being indicted on charges related to Cuba's deadly 1996 shootdown of planes operated by humanitarian group Brothers to the Rescue, U.S. officials told CBS News earlier this month.

item.title: WHO says Ebola risks high for region, but not a "pandemic emergency"
item.description: WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus says risks from the Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda are "high at the national and regional levels, and low at the global level."

item.title: Suspected drug trafficker rescued from plane crash linked to politician
item.description: A survivor of a recent plane crash near Florida was allegedly found with roughly $30,000 inside a bag labeled with the name of a Bahamian politician.

item.title: The major cases the Supreme Court will decide in the coming weeks
item.description: The Supreme Court's term is set to end around the end of June, with decisions on birthright citizenship, transgender athlete bans and gun rights still to come.

item.title: Cheaper, alternative health plans are having a moment, but critics urge caution
item.description: Congress' decision not to extend enhanced marketplace tax credits has boosted the appeal of alternative health coverage with lower monthly premiums.

item.title: As gas prices surge, consumers search for other savings
item.description: The national average for a gallon of gasoline has been ticking up since the start of the war with Iran. The latest average of $4.50 a gallon is an increase of over $1.50 since the war started.
